title: The Framework Landscape
intro: There are dozens of JavaScript frameworks out there. Picking one is like choosing a vehicle — you want something the right size for the job, not a semi-truck for a grocery run. Swipe right on smart framework-picking strategies, left on common mistakes beginners make when choosing one.

teach.title: Frameworks Are Tools, Not Tribes
teach.body: React, Vue, Svelte, Angular — they all build UIs. The best framework is the one that fits your project's needs, has good docs, and has a community you can lean on when you're stuck. Don't pick a framework because of Twitter hype.

teach.title: Picking a Framework = Picking a Vehicle
teach.body: Building a task manager with Angular is like driving a semi-truck to the grocery store. It works, but it's overkill. You want something the right size for the job — powerful enough to handle your needs, simple enough that you won't get lost.

teach.title: The Power Tool Aisle
teach.body: Walking into a hardware store and buying the most expensive drill doesn't make you a better builder. What matters is: does the drill fit the screws you're using? Does it come with the bits you need? Can you find replacement parts easily? Frameworks are the same — the 'best' one is the one that fits YOUR project and has the support YOU need.

Choosing the right framework from day one means you can build and iterate faster. Faster iteration means faster product-market fit, and that's how solo builders turn side projects into income streams.
Choose a framework based on: project complexity, community size, available learning resources, and how well it matches what you're building. Ignore hype cycles.

cards.content: Pick a framework with a large community so you can find answers when you get stuck
cards.explanation: A large community means more tutorials, Stack Overflow answers, and third-party libraries. When you hit a wall, someone has already solved your problem.

cards.content: Always pick the newest framework because it must be the best
cards.explanation: New frameworks often have incomplete docs, fewer tutorials, and breaking changes. Maturity matters — especially when you're learning.

cards.content: Consider what your project needs before picking a framework — a static blog has different needs than a real-time app
cards.explanation: Different frameworks excel at different things. Matching the tool to the job saves you from fighting the framework later.

cards.content: A framework got 10,000 likes on Twitter this week, so switch your project to it immediately
cards.explanation: Social media hype is not a technical evaluation. A trending tweet tells you nothing about whether a framework fits your task manager.

cards.content: Read the framework's official documentation and try the getting-started tutorial before committing
cards.explanation: Docs quality directly impacts your speed. If the docs are confusing before you start, they'll be worse when you're debugging at 2 AM.

cards.content: Pick a framework you've never heard of just because a job posting mentioned it once
cards.explanation: Resume-driven development leads to poor choices. Learn tools because they solve your problem, not to pad your LinkedIn.

cards.content: Check if the framework has good support for things you'll need, like routing, auth, and database integration
cards.explanation: A task manager needs routing, forms, and data fetching at minimum. Checking the ecosystem upfront prevents painful workarounds later.
